title: Hearing Protection Act
stage_label: Referred to the Committee on Ways and Means, and in addition to the Committee on the Judiciary, for a period to be subse
plain_summary: This bill would change the rules for buying silencers, which are devices that make guns quieter. Right now, people have to pay extra taxes and fill out lots of paperwork to buy a silencer, but this bill would get rid of those requirements. Instead, buying a silencer would work just like buying a regular gun, with only a ten percent sales tax added. The government would also have to destroy the records they keep about silencers that people already own. States would not be allowed to make their own registration systems for silencers.
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Ways and Means, and in addition to the Committee on the Judiciary, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provis

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Hearing Protection Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Equal Treatment of Silencers and Firearms
sections.text: This section changes the tax law to remove silencers from the list of items that require special federal taxes and paperwork. Silencers are devices that reduce the noise when a gun is fired. Right now, silencers are treated the same as machine guns and other heavily regulated weapons under federal tax law. This change would make silencers subject to the same rules as regular guns instead. The new rules would start ninety days after this law is passed.

sections.title: Section 3  Treatment of Certain Silencers
sections.text: This section says that if someone gets or owns a silencer following the regular federal gun laws, they automatically meet any registration and licensing requirements under the National Firearms Act. The National Firearms Act is the law that currently requires special paperwork and taxes for certain weapons. This means people would not need to do the extra paperwork that is currently required for silencers.

sections.title: Section 4  Preemption of Certain State Laws in Relation to Firearm Silencers
sections.text: This section stops state and local governments from creating their own special taxes, record keeping requirements, or registration systems for silencers. States can still charge regular sales tax on silencers like they do for other products. However, states cannot create special silencer taxes or require people to register silencers with the state if those silencers are being made, sold, used, owned, or moved between states or to other countries.

sections.title: Section 5  Destruction of Records
sections.text: Within one year after this law passes, the Attorney General must destroy all government records about silencers. This includes any registration records for silencers in the National Firearms Registration and Transfer Record, any applications to transfer silencers to new owners, and any applications to make silencers. The National Firearms Registration and Transfer Record is the government database that tracks certain regulated weapons.

sections.title: Section 6  Amendments to Title 18 United States Code
sections.text: This section changes federal gun laws to include silencers and mufflers as types of firearms that can be sold to adults. It creates a clear definition of what counts as a firearm silencer or muffler. These devices are designed to make guns quieter when fired and can be attached directly to the gun or through adapters. The section also requires that silencers and mufflers have serial numbers marked on their main housing or structure, just like regular guns do. If marking the main part is not practical, manufacturers can ask the Attorney General for permission to mark it somewhere else. The section treats silencers and mufflers the same as regular guns when it comes to age restrictions for sales.

sections.title: Section 7  Imposition of Tax on Firearm Silencers or Firearm Mufflers
sections.text: This section creates a new ten percent tax on silencers and mufflers when they are sold by manufacturers. This is similar to the tax already placed on other gun equipment. The tax applies to any device designed to make portable guns quieter. The new tax rules start ninety days after this law is passed. This section also updates other tax rules to include silencers and mufflers in the same category as other firearms equipment.
